title: Riven cherry carving mallet
subtitle: A green cherry mallet head for driving gouges and chisels
excerpt: A carving mallet head riven from green cherry: round-sectioned, barrel-shaped, 10 cm in diameter and 15 cm long. Cherry is dense enough for driving chisels but lighter than beech or oak; the weight is well-suited to a carving mallet that is swung frequently. The handle is a turned ash or hazel shaft.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. II (1885) for mallet construction; carving mallet forms are documented in Victorian and Edwardian craft manuals including Newey and Drage.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. The mallet head is shaped green and dries to full hardness on the handle.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul. A riven mallet head is stronger than a sawn blank; the grain runs through it without interruption and the face will not split under repeated striking.
A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece. Used at the shaving horse to rough the mallet head to a cylinder.
A foot-pedalled bench that clamps the workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.

content.text: A carving mallet is a tool that most carvers make; the commercial alternatives are either too heavy (beech and ash mallets from the joinery tradition) or too light (rubber mallets). A
riven

cherry head weighs 350 to 450 grams depending on the final dimensions; enough to drive a gouge through seasoned lime with a firm stroke but light enough to use for several hours without arm fatigue. The head is
green
when shaped, which makes the drawknife work easy, and it dries to its final density in the finished mallet.
Grip: both hands on the drawknife handle; foot pressure on the shaving horse pedal clamps the work. Never make a drawknife stroke that travels toward a body part not protected by the clamp.
Froe and beetle: keep the off-hand clear of the blow line. One beetle stroke per froe position is enough to start a cherry split.
Sharpening: strop the drawknife and spokeshave every 15 minutes.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach.
Workspace: stable shaving horse on a level floor.
The wood

A short section of green cherry branch: 20 cm long, 14 cm in diameter. Wild cherry and orchard cherry both work; wild cherry is denser and harder. The pith should run through the centre; avoid a section where the pith is off-centre, as the head will dry unevenly. A straight-grained section is essential; grain that runs at an angle through the head will split at the struck face over time.
Method
Rive the section in half along the pith line with the froe and beetle. Each half yields one mallet head blank.
Mount the half-section in the
shaving horse
and remove the corners with the
drawknife
to produce an octagonal cross-section. Target: 12 cm across the flats.

Refine to a cylinder with the spokeshave, working around the head from one end. The cylinder should be 10 to 11 cm in diameter. Remove material from the striking faces at each end to produce a slight barrel shape; the centre of the head 1 cm larger in diameter than the ends. The barrel shape distributes the striking force across the face rather than concentrating it at the rim.

Bore the handle mortise through the pith axis; 25 mm diameter, slightly angled so the handle is at 3 to 5 degrees from the head axis (this keeps the striking face square when the mallet is swung). Bore with a brace and bit, or a drill, from both ends to prevent break-out.

Turn or shave the ash or hazel handle to a taper: 25 mm at the shoulder to 18 mm at the grip end, 30 cm long. Drive the handle through the mortise from the top; the taper should bind at the shoulder. Saw a wedge slot across the handle end protruding above the head; drive a dry hardwood wedge to lock the handle in the mortise.
Leave to dry 3 to 4 weeks. The cherry head will shrink onto the handle taper and the joint will tighten. Check the wedge after 2 weeks.
Finishing
Apply raw linseed oil to the handle and the barrel sides; not to the striking faces, which must remain bare wood. A linseed-oiled face will split rather than dent on impact.
Variations
Birch head: lighter; better for fine work or where arm fatigue is a concern. Birch dries faster than cherry; check the handle fit at 2 weeks.
Larger head: 14 cm diameter for a joinery mallet that drives mortise chisels through oak.
Care

If the head loosens after years of use, remove it, re-drive the wedge, or insert a second smaller wedge at 90 degrees to the first. A mallet head that spins on the handle is driven off the handle under impact; check the joint annually.

items.symptom: Head cracks across the striking face during drying
items.cause: Grain running at an angle through the head section
items.fix: The crack is structural; the mallet is lost. Select a new section with straight grain confirmed by reading the side of the billet before riving.

items.symptom: Handle loose immediately after driving the wedge
items.cause: Handle taper too slight; insufficient mechanical advantage
items.fix: Re-cut the wedge deeper or drive a second wedge. The taper on the handle shoulder must be steep enough that the head cannot move back up the handle under its own weight when inverted.
